title: [HOLD] Cirrus: Use containers for more tasks
body:
Depends on: #14016

Containers are vastly faster to create and use, since they don't need to
"boot".  The automation_images repo. ensures the images are
substantially similar to the VMs in terms of packages and setup.
Previously, these container images could not be widely used in CI
because Cirrus-CI does not support sharing task-cache between VMs
& containers. Artifacts & curl don't have this limitation, allowing
wider use of containers in podman CI.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
None
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich* To complete the [pull request process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process), please assign **edsantiago** after the PR has been reviewed. You can assign the PR to them by writing `/assign @edsantiago` in a comment when ready. The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

***Review note***: We may not want to merge this, and just abandon it.  I noticed container-tasks were queuing due to load on the community cluster.  I haven't examined task-runtimes closely vs #14016 (global runtime is useless due to re-runs), but suspect some things ran much slower.  While using containers for tasks is generally desirable, and will help future task consolidation and simplification, the actual impact to developer-time may be negative.  The only solution I'm aware of to eliminate community-cluster queuing risk, is to stand up [a managed GKE cluster and have cirrus point at that](https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/supported-computing-services/#kubernetes-engine) (docs) for container tasks.

> I assume this is the problem you mentioned in demos this morning, and the reason you're holding?

Yes exactly, and it's utterly unpredictable when/how it will impact us from moment to moment. :sob: The only way around it I'm afraid is to throw money at a managed GKE (a competitor of OpenShift) cluster.  Best case that means two persistent VMs (managed by google), the worst-case of n-VMs is a limit we can set ourselves (i.e. in case we notice container-task queuing).  I suspect the maintenance overhead this would add is minimal, but as you said it may not be worth it for a few minutes saved per PR.

> two removals from get_vi_vm.sh which I suspect should've been in a separate commit

Yeah they should, but they're pretty minor updates so I'm not afraid if they get lost.

In case it's helpful, lately I've been using GH actions for more things.  There's a lot of zero-cost efficient CI capacity there.

And specifically, it's very convenient to execute a privileged container from inside the default Ubuntu VM.  See https://github.com/ostreedev/ostree-rs-ext/blob/d48e462c42eee053c53d40f9ab907688b5e54c0a/.github/workflows/rust.yml#L146
for example.

> In case it's helpful, lately I've been using GH actions for more things.

It's a helpful suggestion.  Many of the would-be container tasks are dependencies for other Cirrus tasks.  So the challenge is getting the two systems working together.  GHA really wants to be the "Uber CI", so it's hard to synchronize a third-party tool with it.  Perhaps unsurprisingly, it works fine the other way around (GHA waiting for Cirrus).  Anyway, the "Easy Button" here (by far) is to just click the GKE or EKS button.  The danger is all the unknown long-term TODO's that implies.

> Anyway, the "Easy Button" here (by far) is to just click the GKE or EKS button. 

There's also the Prow instance we use for OCP...
I'm going to close this.  I don't see any solution that is simple enough in relation to the amount of expected potential time savings.
